title: Named cancellable timers and anonymous sleeps
purpose: Provide the engine-side timer API the SDK binds to: start_timer for author-named, cancellable timers; cancel_timer to retire one; and sleep for anonymous delays with an engine-assigned TimerId. These are the verbs a workflow author reaches for (deadlines, SLAs, delays), built on the AT-002 TimerService.
task: Implement start_timer, cancel_timer, and sleep on top of TimerService. start_timer takes an author-assigned TimerId and schedules a durable cancellable timer. cancel_timer disarms the wheel entry via the seam and records TimerCancelled, treating an already-fired timer as a no-op. sleep schedules an anonymous timer whose TimerId is engine-assigned from sequence position and is not separately cancellable.

requirements.title: start_timer (named, cancellable)
requirements.spec: WHEN start_timer is called with an author-assigned TimerId and a fire_at/duration, THE SYSTEM SHALL schedule a durable, cancellable timer via TimerService.
acceptance: named.rs defines start_timer(workflow_id, timer_id, fire_at) that delegates to TimerService::schedule (record + arm).
The TimerId is the author-assigned one (named timer), preserved verbatim in the TimerStarted event and the schedule_timer record.
A test calls start_timer with a named TimerId and asserts a TimerStarted event with that exact id and the timer is retrievable via expired_timers at/after fire_at.

requirements.title: cancel_timer
requirements.spec: WHEN cancel_timer is called for a named timer that has not fired, THE SYSTEM SHALL disarm the wheel entry via the seam AND append a TimerCancelled event. IF the timer has already fired, THEN cancel_timer SHALL be a no-op and SHALL NOT return an error.
acceptance: cancel_timer(workflow_id, timer_id) disarms the wheel entry (seam disarm) and appends TimerCancelled { timer_id }.
If the timer already has a recorded TimerFired, cancel_timer returns Ok without appending TimerCancelled and without disarming (no-op).
If the timer already has a recorded TimerCancelled, cancel_timer is an idempotent no-op.
A test starts a named timer, cancels it, and asserts a TimerCancelled event is recorded and the wheel entry was disarmed.
A test fires a timer then cancels it and asserts cancel is a no-op (no TimerCancelled, no error).

requirements.title: sleep (anonymous, engine-assigned id)
requirements.spec: WHEN sleep is called for a duration, THE SYSTEM SHALL schedule an anonymous durable timer with an engine-assigned TimerId derived from the workflow's sequence position. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT expose a separate cancel for an anonymous sleep.
acceptance: sleep(workflow_id, duration) computes fire_at from the workflow's recorded time + duration and schedules via TimerService with an engine-assigned TimerId derived from sequence position (deterministic, so replay matches the same TimerId).
The anonymous TimerId derivation is deterministic given the workflow's sequence position (documented), so AD's replay reconstructs the same id.
No public cancel entrypoint accepts an anonymous-sleep timer specifically (cancelling a sleep is cancelling the workflow); doc comment states this.
A test calls sleep, asserts a TimerStarted event with an engine-derived id and the correct fire_at relative to the workflow's recorded time.

boundaries: SHALL NOT reimplement scheduling, arming, or firing — those are TimerService (AT-002).
SHALL NOT implement recovery — that is AT-003.
SHALL NOT define the Gleam workflow.sleep/start_timer/cancel_timer surface — that is AF; this brief is the engine-side mechanism it binds to.
SHALL NOT derive fire_at from the wall clock — it uses the workflow's recorded time for determinism.
verification: cargo test -p aion passes, including the cancel no-op tests.
Anonymous TimerId derivation is deterministic (no random/UUID for anonymous sleeps).
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.
